title: The mistake letter ritual
subtitle: A ten-minute writing ritual for releasing guilt and regret around a past money decision.
excerpt: A ten-minute ritual for the money decision that still replays. Write a compassionate letter to the version of you who made it, what they knew at the time, what they were working with, what you'd say if it were a friend. Then read it back and release it. Paper, pen, ten minutes alone.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 5 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Release shame about past mistakes." Compassionate letter-writing as a self-inquiry tool is documented across cognitive-behavioural and narrative therapy traditions. The ceremonial release element (burning or tearing the letter) is a public-domain ritual form. The five-part Prepare / Release / Allow / Integrate / Anchor structure is Rebecca J Page's framework from The Money Journal (2025).

content.text: A ten-minute writing ritual for the money decision that still replays. Write a compassionate letter to the version of you who made it, what they were working with at the time, what they knew, what you'd say if a friend described the same situation. Read it back once. Then release it. Paper, pen, ten minutes alone.
Paper and pen, handwritten only. Typing doesn't carry the same ceremonial weight.
A way to release the letter at the end: burn it safely, tear it into small pieces, or seal it in an envelope to keep. Choose before you start.

Sit down. Choose the one specific decision you're addressing, the period of overspending, the investment that went wrong, the loan that cost more than expected. Name it to yourself. Pick one; not the whole list.

attrs.quote: I am ready to release the shame I carry around this decision. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.

content.text: Address the letter to the version of you who made the decision. Start with: "At the time, you were working with..". Then write what was true then: the information you had, the pressure you were under, the stage of life, the beliefs about money that were operating. Write what a kind and informed friend would say, not what the inner critic says now.
Five minutes. No corrections, no crossing out. Let it be imperfect.
Read the letter once, slowly. Don't edit. If something makes you emotional, let it. Notice which line lands hardest, that's the one the old guilt was sitting on.

attrs.quote: I allow forgiveness, growth, and a clean start with money.

content.text: Then: burn the letter safely if you have somewhere to do it, tear it into small pieces and bin them, or seal it in an envelope and put it away. Whichever you chose before you started. The act of disposal is the ritual close. It signals that the review is done.
If the guilt loop runs too fast to write from a compassionate place, tap first.
clears the charge before you try to write. Come back to this ritual once the loop has loosened.

Adapted from Day 5 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The focus of the day is releasing shame around past money choices. Compassionate letter-writing as a tool of self-inquiry runs across cognitive-behavioural and narrative therapy traditions. The ceremonial release element is a public-domain ritual form. The five-part ritual structure is from The Money Journal (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
